title: CMake project structure

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for CMake project structure; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: C++ Core Guidelines; living guidelines accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to CMake project structure
sources.claim: A reliable C++ build pins language mode and toolchain, tracks header and generated dependencies, applies consistent ABI flags, and links one ODR-coherent program.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A reliable C++ build pins language mode and toolchain, tracks header and generated dependencies, applies consistent ABI flags, and links one ODR-coherent program.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A changed class header recompiles every dependent translation unit before tests link against one consistent layout. Your goal is to configure and build a multi-target CMake project without global mutable build settings. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Conceptual constraints and diagnostics; Exceptions and guarantees; Headers and source separation; Compile and link model. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A reliable C++ build pins language mode and toolchain, tracks header and generated dependencies, applies consistent ABI flags, and links one ODR-coherent program. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Compiler and standard-library ABI, modules, precompiled headers, build caches, conditional macros, and platform libraries can invalidate otherwise successful incremental output. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Object files built with any compiler flags can be mixed safely as long as the linker accepts their names.

includes: Declare targets, include paths, warnings and test execution reproducibly.
Configure and build a multi-target CMake project without global mutable build settings.
excludes: Compiler and standard-library ABI, modules, precompiled headers, build caches, conditional macros, and platform libraries can invalidate otherwise successful incremental output.

outcomes: Configure and build a multi-target CMake project without global mutable build settings.

examples.title: CMake project structure: defined case
examples.situation: A changed class header recompiles every dependent translation unit before tests link against one consistent layout.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A changed class header recompiles every dependent translation unit before tests link against one consistent layout.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Keep ABI-affecting compiler, library, macro, and language settings consistent across every linked translation unit and dependency.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Keep ABI-affecting compiler, library, macro, and language settings consistent across every linked translation unit and dependency.

examples.title: CMake project structure: boundary case
examples.situation: CI performs a clean C++20 build with the same warning and sanitizer configuration documented for local development.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: CI performs a clean C++20 build with the same warning and sanitizer configuration documented for local development.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Compiler and standard-library ABI, modules, precompiled headers, build caches, conditional macros, and platform libraries can invalidate otherwise successful incremental output.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Object files built with any compiler flags can be mixed safely as long as the linker accepts their names.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Configure and build a multi-target CMake project without global mutable build settings.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Object files built with any compiler flags can be mixed safely as long as the linker accepts their names.
misconceptions.correction: Keep ABI-affecting compiler, library, macro, and language settings consistent across every linked translation unit and dependency.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: Object files built with any compiler flags can be mixed safely as long as the linker accepts their names.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model CMake project structure: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Compiler and standard-library ABI, modules, precompiled headers, build caches, conditional macros, and platform libraries can invalidate otherwise successful incremental output.

recap.summary: You should now be able to configure and build a multi-target CMake project without global mutable build settings. Keep this boundary in view: Compiler and standard-library ABI, modules, precompiled headers, build caches, conditional macros, and platform libraries can invalidate otherwise successful incremental output.
